---
label: "Gateway Architecture"
icon: "🏗️"
---

## Gateway Architecture

An LLM gateway can be deployed in three architectural patterns: **reverse proxy**, **sidecar**, or **embedded SDK**. Each pattern has different implications for <JargonTip term="latency">latency</JargonTip>, operational complexity, and feature richness.

  <ComparisonTable title="Gateway Deployment Patterns" columns={['Pattern','How It Works','Latency Impact','Best For']} rows={[
    ['Reverse Proxy','Standalone service between app and LLM providers','Adds network hop (1-5ms)','Multi-service architectures, centralized control'],
    ['Sidecar','Co-located process alongside each app instance','Minimal (~0.5ms)','Kubernetes deployments, per-service policies'],
    ['Embedded SDK','Library imported directly into application code','Zero network overhead','Simple apps, serverless, edge deployments'],
  ]}/>
  <CodeBlock b64="" />
  <Quiz question="When should you choose a sidecar gateway over a reverse proxy?" options={["Single monolithic app","Kubernetes with per-service routing policies and minimal latency","Simplest possible setup","Deploying to serverless"]} correctIndex={1} explanation="The sidecar pattern excels in Kubernetes where each service needs its own gateway instance co-located in the same pod. This minimizes latency while allowing per-service routing policies and rate limits."/>
